name: tikka masala
instructions:
TWO-PART RECIPE: the sauce and the protein, prepared in parallel.

SAUCE: build a punjabi masala — saute red onions and garlic in vegetable oil and ghee, add canned tomatoes and tomato paste, add the ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, cardamom, kashmiri chili powder, amchur powder), simmer until reduced. blend smooth (the sauce is not chunky). finish by stirring in cream — either heavy whipping cream OR coconut milk/cream (see notes for the tradeoff).

PROTEIN: cut chicken breast into small chunks BEFORE marinating. marinate the protein in yogurt with toasted ground spices for a few hours (the spices should be toasted dry, without oil, before grinding). broil the marinated chunks until done.

Finish by combining the broiled protein with the sauce; stir in kasoori methi at the end.
notes:
TWO PARTS: a sauce + a marinated protein, prepared separately and combined at the end. Each part has its own failure modes.

THE SAUCE: it's a punjabi masala (tomatoes, onion, garlic) blended smooth with cream. the main decision axis is which cream you use:
- HEAVY CREAM tastes a bit better in isolation — richer, rounder.
- COCONUT MILK/CREAM keeps the sauce's red color. tomato paste, canned tomatoes, and kashmiri chili powder produce a rich red, but dairy cream turns the whole sauce yellow/sickly. coconut preserves the red.
- Two reasons to pick coconut even though dairy tastes marginally better: (1) the plate may already be overloaded with cream and dairy (common in indian meals — naan with butter, raita, paneer dishes alongside), so leaning coconut here gives variety; (2) visual appeal — tikka masala is supposed to be red.

TOMATO PASTE: load-bearing for depth and color. Don't skip it — canned tomatoes alone won't give you the concentrated red you want, especially when paired with coconut.

THE PROTEIN: chicken and tofu both work, but they have different failure modes.

CHICKEN FAILURE MODE: cut the chicken breast into small chunks BEFORE marinating and broiling. If you're lazy (don't want to dirty a cutting board with raw chicken), the chunks will be too large and cook unevenly under the broiler — the yogurt marinade complicates broiler timing, and large pieces leave you with under-done centers and over-charred exteriors.

TOFU FAILURE MODE: press firm tofu in the fridge for several hours before marinating. This drives off the aquafaba and gives you a dry, tasty tofu that takes the yogurt marinade properly. You cannot cheat by boiling — boiled tofu is too soft and wet, and the yogurt marinade interacts poorly with the moisture. You also cannot substitute extra/super firm tofu to skip the press step; the factory-pressed version just doesn't taste as good as home-pressed firm tofu (covered in the tofu article).

GHEE IN THE SAUCE: ghee in the sauce sauté (alongside vegetable oil) helps a lot with flavor — gives the punjabi base a depth and richness that's hard to replicate with neutral oil alone. Worth keeping on hand for indian cooking specifically.

YOGURT: plain yogurt is preferred over greek. Plain yogurt makes a smoother marinade — it needs less oil to thin into a liquid that coats the protein evenly. Greek yogurt is thicker and needs more oil to get to the same consistency. Plain yogurt is also tangier and tastes more 'indian' in the finished dish. If you only have greek on hand, add a squeeze of lemon juice to compensate for the missing tang.

KASOORI METHI: non-optional. Stirred in at the end. This is the herb that makes tikka masala taste like tikka masala rather than a generic tomato-cream curry.

VEGETABLES DO NOT BELONG: this is a sauce + marinated meat dish, not a vegetable curry. Broccoli, cauliflower, and other vegetables are red herrings here — they don't belong in tikka masala.
